observations: Client sent 304 protected data frame(s) — ALL non-unicast: 223 IPv6 multicast (33:33:*/DAD/RS/mDNS), 60 IPv4 multicast (01:*/mDNS/SSDP), 21 broadcast (ff:ff:*/ARP/DHCP). Zero unicast data sent by client = DHCP/IP assignment failed
AP sent 14 protected unicast frame(s) to client after handshake
372 QoS-Null power-save poll(s) from client — AP repeatedly has no downlink data queued for client
Non-unicast-only traffic pattern (IPv6 DAD/RS/mDNS + ARP/DHCP broadcasts) confirms client never received an IP address — typical L3 connectivity failure after successful 802.11+WPA2 auth

ip_connectivity_failure.message: 1 session(s) where WPA2 handshake succeeded but AP sent no unicast data — likely DHCP/IP connectivity failure (client: 00:04:ea:23:46:8c, AP: fa:90:f2:48:e0:75)
root_cause_candidates: DHCP server not responding or unreachable from AP
AP has no uplink / internet gateway is down
Client IP address pool exhausted on AP
VLAN or firewall policy blocking client after auth
AP in isolated BSS mode (no L3 routing)
recommended_actions: Check DHCP server status and available lease pool on AP
Verify AP uplink/WAN port and gateway connectivity
Check for VLAN assignment or firewall rules applied post-auth
Test by connecting another device to the same SSID
Review AP system logs for DHCP relay or IP assignment errors

issues: WPA3-SAE + 4-way EAPOL handshake completed successfully (Msg1–Msg4 all seen, 2 SAE Confirm(s) with status=0).

5 auth frame(s) used Open/WPA2 auth (auth_alg=0) while SAE (WPA3) is also advertised in the BSS RSN IE. This indicates the BSS is running in WPA3 Transition Mode (mixed WPA2+WPA3). Ensure WPA3-only clients connect with SAE; legacy WPA2 clients will use PSK. To enforce WPA3-only: disable WPA2-PSK on the AP once all clients support SAE.
wpa3_sae_failures.message: WPA3-SAE analysis: 1 failure event(s) — 1× WPA3 transition mode — some clients using WPA2

high_retry_rate.message: High retry rate: 35.7% of data frames are retries (threshold: 15%)

issues.description: 346 frame(s) with NAV > 5ms (max: 32690 μs). Excessively large Duration/NAV values can starve other stations from accessing the medium (virtual carrier sense abuse).
issues.impact: Other stations defer transmission for the NAV duration, causing unfair medium access and throughput starvation.
issues.remediation: Identify source of excessive NAV values (check TA field). Update driver/firmware. If intentional: possible NAV attack — enable AP-side protection.

control_frame_issues.message: Control frame analysis: 1 issue(s) — 2850 RTS, 5244 CTS, 3 PS-Poll, 68 BAR

issues.issue: Client polling with no AP data response
issues.description: Client 00:04:ea:2c:58:b1 sent 416 Null/QoS-Null poll(s) but AP sent no data frames back. Client is repeatedly waking from power-save to check for buffered data, but AP has nothing queued.
issues.impact: Wasted airtime and client battery on futile PS-poll cycles.
issues.remediation: If client expects data: check AP uplink connectivity and DHCP/IP status. If client is idle: increase DTIM period to reduce wakeup frequency. Check AP is correctly buffering multicast/broadcast for PS clients.

issues.description: Client e4:b3:18:a2:63:13 had 768 power-save mode transitions across 5629 frames (13.6% of frames are PS boundary changes). Rapid PS transitions cause latency spikes.
issues.impact: Each PS transition adds latency (50-200ms for buffered frames). Rapid cycling wastes battery and airtime.
issues.remediation: Configure client to use WMM-PS (U-APSD) instead of legacy PS-Poll. Adjust PS wake interval in client driver. For latency-sensitive apps: disable power-save entirely.
